title: This Is How They Tell Me the World Ends

chapters.title: A new normal in the connected world
chapters.text:
Ukraine declared independence from the Soviet Union through a referendum, but many people have long suspected outside hands in the blackouts that hit Ukrainian cities.

The attack knocked out railways, ATMs, gas stations, the postal service, and the radiation monitors at the Chornobyl nuclear site. It all started after Ukraine rose up against the Russian government in 2014. Viktor Yanukovych, then the president, was pushed out of office and plans moved forward to elect a Western leaning leader. The vote was hit by glitches that looked like Russian fingerprints.

A cyberattack can take down anything that runs on a network.

No paper trail ties the Kremlin to hackers waging cyberwar against Ukraine, the United States, and other rivals, but the body language of President Putin gave those freelancers cover. In a striking twist, Putin praised Russian hackers as patriotic citizens and promised to call on them when needed.

Cyberattacks from China, the United States, Russia, and North Korea point to a new shape of war. America intelligence chiefs have found real threats aimed at the power grid, online retail, and other digital assets. The worry in the United States has two roots. Ukraine has only a thin slice of its economy online, and Russia still managed to freeze Kyiv. Imagine what would happen to a country that runs on the internet if it took a hit like that.

This summary will walk you through a cast of characters and a set of stories about how different groups race to grab and hold the internet. You will get an unusual look at the people who shape the web.

chapters.title: A journey into the cave of secrets
chapters.text:
Burned out from writing about cybersecurity for the Times, reporter Nicole Perlroth took a break in Kenya. She spent time in rural parts of the country, away from her laptop.

Three weeks into the trip, she drove back to Nairobi, where the cell signal was strong, and checked her phone. As expected, the inbox was full. One message jumped out. Her editor at the Times needed her back urgently.

Once she was back, the new assignment was to work with the Guardian and ProPublica on two stories built on the Snowden leaks.

Edward Snowden, a former contractor for the National Security Agency, leaked documents that showed how wide NSA surveillance had grown.

The two stories Perlroth helped write were about what the NSA could do and how well spy agencies broke digital codes.

In her reporting, Perlroth learned that the NSA often pulled data through back channels, hacking the inside servers of big tech firms like Yahoo. Back channeling, in the cybersecurity world, means passing information through hidden routes that attackers or defenders set up.

Hackers find back channels by spotting software flaws. These flaws are called zero days because the software maker has had no time to fix them before they are used.

Both criminals and defenders hunt for zero days, and the hunt has fueled many attacks. Chinese hackers, for example, stole some of the most guarded source code in Silicon Valley by riding these flaws.

Back to the NSA story. Perlroth and her team realized the copy of the Snowden documents they had was not enough to write the full picture, so they kept digging.

After months of working alone, for safety, the team was dismayed to find they had been used. The British side published their own stories without crediting Perlroth or anyone else on the Times team. The Guardian had wanted the Times team along as cover against British intelligence, not as partners.

chapters.title: Lone players with global reach
chapters.text:
Almost everyone Perlroth talked to at the start of her reporting on the zero day market told her it was a wild goose chase. Even governments were not in charge of zero days. They were just one set of buyers among many. That meant a government had no reason to spill its zero day secrets to a reporter. Perlroth pressed on. She had a knack for the work and found a few zero day traders.

One key contact was John P. Watters, a businessman with a sharp eye. During the dot com bust around 1999, Watters picked up a struggling cybersecurity firm called iDefense for a tiny sum. Yes, ten dollars. With the small staff left in place, including two young hackers named David Endler and Sunil James, Watters tried to turn the company around.

The plan the two hackers pitched was simple. Pay outside hackers for zero day finds, then warn iDefense clients before the software maker shipped a patch. The plan was a gamble, but the bet paid off. After running the firm for three years, Watters sold iDefense to VeriSign for forty million dollars.

Keep good people on staff and give them room to share ideas.

Perlroth also met a middleman she called Jimmy Sabien. Sabien was not a company builder, but he brokered deals between governments and hackers, and between security firms and the people who wanted bugs kept quiet.

The old rule about an eye for an eye leaves everyone blind.

Another contact was Charlie Miller, one of the NSA top talent for breaking into networks through bugs and zero days. Miller later went public with what he knew about the cyber arms market, much like Snowden did, but Miller stayed within the rules.

Through these and other sources, Perlroth gathered the material that would shape her reporting on cybersecurity.

chapters.title: A short history of cyber spying
chapters.text:
The use of zero days goes back further than the internet. During the Cold War, the United States worked hard to figure out how the Soviet KGB was pulling diplomatic secrets out of the US embassy in Moscow. The puzzle was that the embassy scrambled every message that left the building. The answer turned out to be hiding in the electric typewriters used by staff. One theory held that a Soviet mole had planted the bug while the typewriters passed through Russian customs. The NSA never proved which theory was right.

The bug let the Soviets read every keystroke and grab messages before they were scrambled.

Data scrambling and cyber spying both predate the internet. The web gave the work a much wider reach.

Planting code inside another country technology is a routine part of the spy game. There is no law against it. The best a country can do is defend its own systems.

The lack of rules does not mean governments admit to spying on each other. Espionage runs on deniability. Officials can always claim they knew nothing.

Organizations cannot stop the world from changing. The smart ones change before they have to.

The United States and Iran offer a clear modern case. In 2008, President George W. Bush signed off on what became the first cyber weapon of mass destruction, a worm known as Olympic Games. The worm slipped into Iran Natanz nuclear plant even though the plant was off the public internet. Within a year, the worm had wrecked two thousand uranium enriching centrifuges. The final payload made the centrifuges spin out of control and wrecked the system.

chapters.title: What big cyber wars mean for everyone
chapters.text:
In December 2009, a serious attack hit Google network. People call it the Aurora attack. Before Google realized it was under fire, US intelligence was already looking at hackers who were hitting companies tied to defense work. The trail led back to senior officials in China.

The two names that came up were Zhou Yongkang, who handled security, and Li Changchun, a powerful figure in charge of propaganda. Secret files showed that the US believed Changchun had been angered by what he read about himself online, and that anger was a likely motive for the attacks.

The Google hack was a big moment. It showed that countries use cyber attacks to fight each other and to shape what people see online. That is a new kind of war, one that runs on computers and networks rather than bullets and tanks. The fear in that picture is that any company, even a giant like Google, can be a target.

Aurora also showed that these online fights are about more than theft or spying. They are about controlling what people read and believe. When a government does not like what is online, it may try to rewrite or hide it. That is a serious problem for free speech and for the way people gather information.

This kind of warfare touches everyone, not only governments or big companies.

The Google story is a warning. It reminds us to protect our computers and our information. We have to stay alert to the countries and hackers who may try to attack us or shape what we see online.

Good computer security and a clear view of what is happening in cyber warfare matter to everyone. Cyber attacks are real, and they can hit anyone who uses the internet.

A small note worth knowing. The people behind the WannaCry ransomware used a tool called EternalBlue, which they had stolen from the NSA.

chapters.title: A cycle of grudges and revenge
chapters.text:
Tensions and payback have been building for years between the big powers online. The fight is not only about viruses and hacks. It is rooted in politics and national interest. Countries now use their cyber talent for both defense and offense, building a new kind of war that is quiet but can be devastating. The United States, Russia, and China are locked in a struggle that uses code and digital tricks to spy, steal, and disrupt.

The United States has long led this digital fight, watching rivals and guarding its own secrets. Other countries are catching up. Russia and China have hacker teams that break into US systems, steal data, and sometimes just stir up trouble to show they can.

In 2016, during the US elections, Russian hackers broke into email systems, stole messages, and leaked them. The goal was not to pick a winner. It was to shake trust in the system.

The fight is not only between countries. Hackers play a big role too. Some work for cash, selling their skills to the highest bidder. Some go after companies or governments they dislike. Some simply enjoy the thrill of breaking into a system no one else can.

This new war is different. It is silent and hidden. You may not know a battle is on until it ends. A power plant goes dark. A bank systems freeze. A government website goes offline. Behind it all, someone somewhere is typing on a keyboard.

The dangers are real. Hospitals lose access to patient records. Banks lose millions. Governments grind to a halt. The scariest part is that we may not know who is behind the attack. Was it a country, a group, or one person? In this shadowy world, it is hard to tell.

Cybersecurity teams push back. They hunt for weaknesses before the bad actors do, and they build digital walls and traps to guard our information.

Hacking is a game of cat and mouse. Attackers find a way in, and defenders close the gap.

This online world is our new reality. We live online, so the fight over this space touches everyone. It is not only about governments and secrets. It is about our personal data, our money, and our safety.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
For years, intelligence agencies hid digital flaws in the name of tracking rivals, planning for war, and protecting national security. That line is wearing thin. The internet has tied us all together in ways we cannot undo, and a flaw that hits one corner of the world can hit all of us. The wall between the physical and the digital is breaking down.

The United States is one of the biggest players in the zero day market, and it has serious financial weight. A new rule that required every zero day dealer or hacker to hand over their tools for patching would shift the norm. It would also discourage dealers from selling the same tools to foreign governments that could use them against the United States, and it would tighten security for everyone.

Countries like the United States need clearer rules. For example, requiring hackers and dealers to share their tools for patching could set a new standard. It would guard against the misuse of those tools and lift the overall level of cybersecurity. The rules would also shape how hackers, brokers, and defense contractors share information with foreign governments. The goal is balance, so that defenders can still talk and act across borders when threats pop up.

Try this. The internet is full of traps, so it pays to be careful about where you click. A few habits help. Keep your privacy settings on. Watch what you download, and avoid strange pop ups. Pick strong passwords. Buy only from secure retail sites. And think about what you post, because it can attract attention you did not bargain for.

You made it to the end. Nice work.
